straight_beat.name: Terrence 'Terry' Boykin
straight_beat.column:
The Orioles won yesterday. That’s the first thing. They beat the White Sox, 5-3, in a game that felt like a step forward, even if the step was smaller than it should have been. Rutschman homered, Bradish pitched into the seventh, and Baltimore beat the Red Sox 4-2 at Camden Yards. Wait, no—that’s yesterday’s game. The White Sox game was yesterday. Let me get that straight. The Orioles are 6-6, two games behind in the AL East. The box, jar, margin.  

The box is Camden Yards. The jar is the bullpen. The margin is the difference between a win and a loss. Or a loss and a win. It’s a narrow margin, and the Orioles keep slipping. They’ve got a .500 record, but

optimist.name: Miz Dottie Hargrove
optimist.column:
The game was a reminder that baseball is a game of patience, and patience, my friends, is a virtue that’s been missing from this town for far too long. Baltimore came out swinging yesterday, not just with the bat but with the kind of heart that makes you believe in the future. Five runs, a bullpen that held its ground, and a young core that’s starting to look like the second coming of ’66—minus the World Series, but with more crab cakes.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: 6-6, two games behind? That’s not exactly a celebration. But let’s not confuse the scoreboard with the soul of this team. These kids—Henderson, Holliday, Rutschman—they’re not here to win the division today. They’re here to build something that’ll outlast the next few seasons. You can’t measure a season by a handful of wins and losses, not when you’ve got a lineup that’s got the fire of a thousand crabs boiling in a pot.  

Take Henderson, for instance. That kid’s got the arm of a pitcher and the heart of a hitter. He’s learning, sure, but learning is what makes legends. Holliday’s got that swagger, the kind that makes you think he’s been around the block a few times. And Rutschman? He’s the kind of kid who’s gonna look back on this season and say, “I remember when I

pessimist.name: Roy Spanulli
pessimist.column:
The Orioles won yesterday. That’s the first thing to note. Five runs, a 3-2 lead in the ninth, a bullpen that finally remembered how to close a game. But here’s the thing: it’s the same old script. Another win, another reminder that this team can still punch above its weight when it wants to. And yet, the record stands at 6-6, a .500 mark that feels like a mirage. The AL East is a gauntlet, but even the most optimistic among us know the division is a sieve.  

The real question isn’t whether the Orioles can win games. It’s whether they can win games consistently enough to matter. Last season, they were a .500 team that made the playoffs. This season, they’re a .500 team that’s already two games behind the .500 team in the division. The math doesn’t add up. You don’t trade
